# Evidence-Based Evolution

## Statement

Standards and governance evolve through logged evidence of real-world pressure,
never through opinion, compromise, or gradual erosion.

## Rationale

Governance systems face two failure modes: rigidity (never changing despite
evidence) and erosion (changing because someone finds it inconvenient). Rigidity
kills by irrelevance — the system becomes a museum piece that everyone routes
around. Erosion kills by entropy — the system loses coherence one exception at a
time until it means nothing.

The solution is a protocol: Log the pressure point → Evaluate the change on its
own merits → Evolve or reject with a recorded decision. "It's always been done
this way" is not a reason to keep something. "It's inconvenient" is not a reason
to change something. Only "would the system independently be better?" qualifies
as a valid input to the decision.

This prevents both ossification and death by a thousand cuts. Every change has a
paper trail. Every rejection has a rationale. The system can explain why it is
the way it is at any point in time — not through institutional memory but through
logged decisions. This is governance that can be audited, not just followed.

## In Practice

The Framework Pressure Protocol is the canonical implementation. When reality
pushes against a standard, the pressure is logged with evidence. The standard is
then evaluated: does the pressure reveal a genuine flaw, or does it reveal that
the standard is doing its job (preventing something that feels convenient but is
actually harmful)?

Standing orders, rules, and standards should all follow the same pattern:
evidence triggers evaluation, evaluation produces evolution or explicit rejection
with rationale. No silent drift. No "we just stopped doing that." No "everyone
agreed it was too hard." Every transition is a governed event with a record.

- JOS-C11 (Evolutionary Optimization)
- JOS-C38 (Epistemic Humility)
- JOS-P08 (Audit and Learn)
